answer:
I think adding new casual words to dictionaries is really important, but it definitely has its pros and cons. On one hand, language is always evolving, right? I mean, when you look at how people communicate daily, especially younger generations, you see that new slang and expressions pop up all the time. For example, words like "lit" or "ghosting" have become part of our everyday vocabulary. By including these terms in dictionaries, we acknowledge that language reflects current culture and trends, which helps keep dictionaries relevant and useful.

However, there are drawbacks too. Some people might argue that adding too many casual words could dilute the richness of the language or lead to misunderstandings. I guess it could make formal writing or communication more confusing if everyone starts using slang in every context. Like, last year, I had a teacher who was super against using slang in essays because she believed it made our arguments less credible. And while I totally get her point, I think recognizing that casual language has its place is equally important.

So, overall, I really believe that updating dictionaries with new casual words can enhance our understanding of language and culture. It promotes inclusivity and shows that language is alive and changing. But of course, we also need to consider contexts where more traditional language is appropriate. Balancing both aspects seems key to me.
title: Should new casual words be added to dictionaries? What are the benefits and drawbacks of doing this?
